title: MPE Tables & AEL Calculations Assessment
description: Test your ability to calculate MPE values, apply correction factors, and determine AEL boundaries

questions.question: Calculate the eye MPE for a 0.25-second exposure to visible laser radiation (point source).
questions.incorrect_feedback: Use the formula MPE = 1.8 × t^0.75 × 10^-3. Remember that 0.25^0.75 = 0.354.

questions.question: Calculate CA for a wavelength of 800 nm.
Why is the CA correction factor needed when calculating MPE for near-IR wavelengths (700-1400 nm)?

options.text: Near-IR lasers are more powerful than visible lasers
The retina is less sensitive to longer wavelengths in this range
Near-IR does not focus on the retina

questions.correct_feedback: Correct! CA accounts for reduced retinal sensitivity at longer wavelengths, allowing proportionally higher MPE values.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Incorrect. CA corrects for the fact that the retina is less sensitive to longer near-IR wavelengths, even though they still focus on the retina.

questions.question: Calculate the eye MPE for a 1-second exposure to an 800 nm laser (point source).
questions.hint: First calculate the base visible MPE, then multiply by CA for 800 nm (CA = 1.58).
questions.incorrect_feedback: Calculate base visible MPE first: 1.8 × 1^0.75 × 10^-3 = 1.8 mJ/cm². Then multiply by CA = 1.58.

questions.question: A laser emits 10,000 pulses. What is the CE correction factor?
questions.correct_feedback: Correct! CE = 10,000^-0.25 = 0.1. Each pulse must be below 10% of single-pulse MPE.

questions.question: What is the approximate Class 3R AEL for a CW 900 nm laser?
questions.correct_feedback: Correct! At 900 nm, CA ≈ 2.5. Class 3R = 5 × Class 1 = 5 × (0.39 × 2.5) ≈ 12.5 mW.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Calculate: CA at 900 nm = 2.5. Class 1 AEL = 0.39 × 2.5 = 0.98 mW. Class 3R = 5 × 0.98 ≈ 12.5 mW.

questions.question: When should the CB correction factor be applied?

options.text: For extended sources with angular size > 1.5 mrad

questions.correct_feedback: Correct! CB applies to extended sources where the apparent angular size exceeds 1.5 mrad, allowing higher total MPE.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Incorrect. CB applies to extended sources (like diffuse reflections) when the apparent angular size α > 1.5 mrad.

questions.question: Calculate CB for a diffuse reflection with an angular size of 15 mrad.
questions.correct_feedback: Correct! CB = 15 / 1.5 = 10. The extended source allows 10× higher MPE.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Use CB = α / α_min where α_min = 1.5 mrad. So CB = 15 / 1.5 = 10.

questions.question: A researcher is exposed to 10 mJ/cm² of 532 nm laser radiation for 1 second. How does this compare to the eye MPE?
questions.correct_feedback: Correct! MPE at 1s, 532 nm = 1.8 mJ/cm². 10/1.8 = 5.5× MPE. This is hazardous.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Calculate MPE = 1.8 × 1^0.75 × 10^-3 = 1.8 mJ/cm². Compare: 10/1.8 = 5.5. This exceeds MPE by factor of 5.5.

questions.question: A 1064 nm laser pointer has an output of 20 mW. What is its IEC classification?
questions.hint: Calculate Class 1 and Class 3R AELs using CA = 5.0 for 1064 nm.
questions.correct_feedback: Correct! Class 1 AEL = 5 × 0.39 = 1.95 mW. Class 3R = 5 × 1.95 = 9.75 mW. 20 mW exceeds Class 3R limit, so it's Class 3B.
questions.incorrect_feedback: Calculate: CA = 5.0. Class 1 = 5×0.39 = 1.95 mW. Class 3R = 5×1.95 = 9.75 mW. 20 mW > 9.75 mW, so Class 3B.
